title: Treacle scones
subtitle: Dark, warmly spiced Scottish scones
excerpt: Treacle scones are a Scottish variety made with black treacle, which gives them a deep, slightly bitter sweetness and a dark crumb. Mixed spice and cinnamon are the usual flavouring. They bake in 12 minutes and are best eaten warm, split and spread with cold butter. Denser than a plain scone and more interesting.
sourceNotes: Treacle scones are a Scottish domestic baking tradition, particularly associated with the baking culture of the Central Belt and the Highlands. Black treacle is a traditional Scottish store-cupboard ingredient used in everything from clootie dumpling to parkin, and its use in scones is well documented in Scottish regional cookery collections from the nineteenth century onward. The combination of treacle with warm baking spices is consistent across historical versions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked scones. Reheat from frozen at 160 degrees C fan for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are made. Freeze any surplus rather than storing overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 10 to 14 minutes until risen and the base sounds hollow when tapped

content.text: Treacle scones follow the same technique as all scones: the fat is rubbed into the flour, the liquid is added, and the dough is handled as little as possible. The treacle is warmed slightly before adding so it mixes into the buttermilk rather than clumping. The dough will be darker than a plain scone dough, noticeably stickier, and the baked scones will be a deep brown rather than golden.

Heat the oven to 210 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift 250 g plain flour, 2 tsp baking powder, half a teaspoon of bicarbonate of soda, 1 tsp mixed spice, half a teaspoon of cinnamon, and a quarter teaspoon of salt into a large bowl. Rub in 50 g cold diced butter until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Stir in 50 g caster sugar. Warm 2 tablespoons of black treacle briefly until just pourable and mix into 150 ml buttermilk. Pour into the flour mixture and stir with a round-bladed knife until a soft dough forms. Turn onto a lightly floured surface and press together gently.

Pat the dough to about 2.5 cm thick. Cut out rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the tray close together. Bake for 10 to 14 minutes until risen, the base sounds hollow when tapped, and the tops look dry. They will be very dark. Leave for 5 minutes before serving.

items.problem: Scones are dense and did not rise
items.solution: The dough was overworked, or the raising agents are old. Handle the dough only enough to bring it together, and use fresh baking powder and bicarbonate of soda.

items.problem: The treacle flavour is too strong
items.solution: Reduce the treacle to 1 tablespoon and increase the caster sugar to 75 g. The recipe is balanced for a pronounced treacle flavour; this adjustment makes it milder.
